questions.q: Which formula adds all numbers in the range A1:A50?
questions.explanation: SUM is the go-to formula for adding numbers. COUNT only counts how many numbers exist — it doesn't add them.
AVERAGE adds all values (10+20+30=60) then divides by how many there are (3), giving 60÷3 = 20.

questions.q: You have a list of expenses in A1:A100, some cells are empty. Which formula counts ONLY the cells with numbers?
questions.explanation: COUNT only counts cells containing numbers, skipping blanks and text. COUNTA would count anything non-empty.

questions.q: Which formula counts ALL non-empty cells, including those with text?
questions.explanation: COUNTA counts any non-empty cell — numbers, text, dates, everything. COUNT only handles numbers.

options: Sums values in B2:B10 only where A2:A10 equals 'Food'
questions.explanation: SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range) sums the sum_range only for rows where the range matches the criteria. Here it totals column B where column A says 'Food'.

questions.q: In SUMIFS, where does the sum_range go?
questions.explanation: SUMIFS(sum_range, range1, criteria1, ...) — the sum_range is always FIRST. This is the opposite of SUMIF where the sum_range is last!

questions.q: You want to sum sales where Region = 'East' AND Amount > 500. Which formula is correct?
questions.explanation: SUMIFS handles multiple conditions: SUMIFS(sum_range, criteria_range1, criteria1, criteria_range2, criteria2, ...). Sum_range (D:D) always comes first.

options: The total of all values in A2:A100
The number of unique (distinct) values in A2:A100
The average frequency of each value
questions.explanation: This is a classic trick: COUNTIF returns how many times each value appears. Dividing 1 by each count, then summing, gives the count of distinct values. If 'Apple' appears 3 times, it contributes 3×(1/3)=1 to the total.

questions.q: Which formula gives a weighted average when you have values in B2:B10 and weights in C2:C10?
questions.explanation: Weighted average = sum of (value × weight) ÷ sum of weights. SUMPRODUCT(values, weights) multiplies each pair and sums them, then divide by SUM(weights).
MIN returns the smallest value. Among 5, 3, 8, and 1, the smallest is 1.
MAX returns the largest value. Among 5, 3, 8, and 1, the largest is 8.
ROUND rounds to the nearest value at 2 decimal places. 3.567 rounds up to 3.57 because the 3rd decimal (7) is >= 5.
LARGE(array, k) returns the k-th largest value. k=1 is the maximum, k=2 is second largest, k=3 is third largest, and so on.
17 divided by 5 = 3 remainder 2. MOD returns the remainder, which is 2.

questions.q: Which is the best way to check if a number is even?
questions.explanation: Both MOD(A2,2)=0 (returns 0 for even) and ISEVEN(A2) (dedicated function) work correctly. Both B and C are valid approaches.

options: Multiplies all of A by all of B
questions.explanation: (A2:A10>5) creates a TRUE/FALSE array. Multiplying by B2:B10 gives B values where condition is true, 0 otherwise. SUMPRODUCT then sums all results — effectively a conditional sum.
ABS returns the absolute value — removing any negative sign. ABS(-42) = 42. ABS(42) also = 42.
ROUNDUP always rounds AWAY from zero, regardless of the decimal value. Even 4.1 becomes 5 when rounding up to 0 decimal places.

questions.q: What does the last argument FALSE mean in =VLOOKUP(A2, B:D, 2, FALSE)?
options: Exact match — don't accept approximate matches
questions.explanation: FALSE = exact match only. TRUE = approximate match (only for sorted data). Always use FALSE unless you specifically need approximate matching on sorted numbers.

questions.q: VLOOKUP always looks in which direction?
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP searches the FIRST column of your range and can only return values to the RIGHT. To look left, use XLOOKUP or INDEX+MATCH.

questions.q: What does XLOOKUP return if it cannot find the value?
questions.explanation: #N/A is the default. But XLOOKUP has a built-in 4th argument for a custom 'not found' message: =XLOOKUP(value, lookup, return, "Not found")

options: The value of the found cell
The position (row number) of the value in the list
The column letter of the match
questions.explanation: MATCH returns the POSITION — a number like 5 meaning 'it's the 5th item'. It doesn't return the value itself. Pair it with INDEX to retrieve the actual value.

questions.q: You want to find the value in column C for the row where column A matches 'Alice'. Which is the correct INDEX+MATCH formula?
questions.explanation: INDEX(where_to_return_from, row_number). MATCH(what_to_find, where_to_look, 0). So: INDEX(C:C, MATCH("Alice", A:A, 0)) — find Alice's position in column A, then return the value at that position in column C.

questions.q: Why would you choose INDEX+MATCH over VLOOKUP?
options: INDEX+MATCH can look left, doesn't break when columns are inserted, and is more flexible
VLOOKUP doesn't work in Google Sheets
questions.explanation: INDEX+MATCH is superior because: it can look in any direction (including left), it references columns directly so inserting columns won't break it, and it's generally faster on large datasets.

questions.q: What does INDIRECT("A"&B1) do when B1 contains the number 5?
options: Looks up row 5 in column B
Returns the value from cell A5
questions.explanation: INDIRECT converts text into a cell reference. "A"&5 = "A5" as text, then INDIRECT goes and fetches the actual value from cell A5.

options: The first row where A = 'East' AND B > 1000, returning column C
All rows where both conditions are met
The sum of C values where A = East
questions.explanation: Multiplying two TRUE/FALSE arrays gives 1 only where BOTH are true. XLOOKUP then finds the first row where the result is 1 — effectively a multi-criteria lookup without helper columns.

options: An error — negative numbers are invalid
questions.explanation: Negative column numbers count from the right. In a 6-column range, -1 = column 6 (last column). So CHOOSECOLS(A1:F10, 1, -1) returns columns A and F.
ROW returns the row number of a cell reference. A5 is in row 5, so ROW(A5) = 5.
COLUMN returns the column number. A=1, B=2, C=3, D=4. So COLUMN(D1) = 4.

questions.q: OFFSET(A1, 2, 3) returns the value from which cell?
questions.explanation: OFFSET starts at A1, moves 2 rows down (to row 3) and 3 columns right (from A to D). Result: cell D3.

questions.q: Why is OFFSET considered 'volatile' and when should you avoid it?
options: It recalculates every time any cell changes, slowing large workbooks
It only works in Excel, not Sheets
It requires the range to be sorted
questions.explanation: Volatile functions (OFFSET, INDIRECT, NOW, TODAY, RAND) recalculate on every single change in the workbook — not just when their inputs change. In large models with many volatile functions, this causes noticeable slowdown. Use INDEX as a non-volatile alternative.

categories.label: ⚖️ Logic & Errors
categories.description: IF, IFS, AND, OR, IFERROR and more

questions.explanation: 5 > 3 is TRUE, so IF returns the first value: "Yes". If the condition were false, it would return "No".

questions.q: A VLOOKUP returns #N/A when the value isn't found. Which formula catches this and shows 'Not Found' instead?
questions.explanation: IFERROR(formula, value_if_error) catches any error and returns the fallback. It's the cleanest way to handle lookup failures.
AND returns TRUE only if ALL conditions are true. Since one is FALSE, the whole AND is FALSE — like a chain where one broken link breaks it all.
OR returns TRUE if ANY condition is true. Just one TRUE is enough. Think of it as 'at least one door is open'.

questions.q: What's the key difference between IFERROR and IFNA?
options: IFNA only catches #N/A errors; IFERROR catches all errors
IFERROR is for Excel only; IFNA is for Sheets only
questions.explanation: IFNA only hides #N/A (not found) errors. IFERROR hides ALL errors. IFNA is safer — if you have a real formula mistake like #REF!, you'll still see it instead of it being silently swallowed.

questions.q: You want to assign grades: A for ≥90, B for ≥80, C for ≥70, F otherwise. The cleanest formula is:
options: Both A and B work, but B is cleaner
questions.explanation: Both nested IF and IFS produce the same result, but IFS is much cleaner and easier to read/maintain. The TRUE at the end acts as the 'else' catch-all.

options: ISERR returns TRUE for all errors EXCEPT #N/A; ISERROR returns TRUE for ALL errors
ISERROR returns TRUE for all errors EXCEPT #N/A; ISERR returns TRUE for ALL errors
questions.explanation: ISERR excludes #N/A from its 'error' check. Use ISERR when #N/A is an expected 'not found' state but you still want to catch formula mistakes like #DIV/0! or #REF!.

questions.q: A cell appears blank but ISBLANK() returns FALSE. What's the likely cause?
options: The cell contains a formula that outputs an empty string ""
The cell has a hidden row
questions.explanation: A cell with =IF(condition, "", value) that evaluates to "" LOOKS empty but actually contains an empty string. ISBLANK only returns TRUE for truly empty cells. Use A2="" to catch both cases.

questions.q: =IF(AND(A2>50, B2="Active"), "Eligible", IF(A2>50, "Pending", "Ineligible")) — what happens when A2=75 and B2="Inactive"?
questions.explanation: A2=75 > 50 is TRUE, but B2="Active" is FALSE, so AND fails → first IF goes to the else branch → second IF: A2>50 is TRUE → returns "Pending".
NOT flips the logical value. NOT(TRUE) = FALSE, NOT(FALSE) = TRUE. Simple reversal.
"42" is text (note the quotes), not a number. ISNUMBER returns FALSE for text. ISNUMBER(42) without quotes would return TRUE.
SWITCH checks the expression (3) against each value in order: 1? No. 2? No. 3? Yes! Returns "Three". The last argument "Other" is the default if nothing matches.

questions.q: When would you use SWITCH instead of IFS?
options: When comparing one value against multiple exact matches
When you need the result to be a number
questions.explanation: SWITCH is perfect for exact value matching (like codes to labels). IFS is better when your conditions involve comparisons (>, <, AND, OR) rather than simple equality checks.
42 is a number, not text. ISTEXT returns FALSE for numbers. ISTEXT("42") with quotes would return TRUE since the quotes make it a text string.

questions.q: Which formula gives you today's date and updates automatically?
questions.explanation: TODAY() returns just the current date and refreshes every time the spreadsheet recalculates. NOW() returns date AND time. TODAY() is better when you only need the date.
Dates in Excel/Sheets are stored as numbers (days since a base date). Adding 7 simply moves forward 7 days. =TODAY()-1 gives yesterday, =TODAY()+30 gives 30 days from now.

options: An actual date value for December 25, 2025
An error — arguments are in wrong order
questions.explanation: DATE(year, month, day) builds a proper date value the spreadsheet understands. It's NOT text — you can do arithmetic on it like =DATE(2025,12,25)+7 to get Jan 1, 2026.

options: Complete years between A2 and today (useful for age)
questions.explanation: DATEDIF with "Y" returns complete years — perfect for calculating age. "M" = months, "D" = days, "YM" = months ignoring years (for showing '35 years, 4 months').
DATE handles overflow automatically! Month 13 of 2025 becomes month 1 of 2026. Similarly, DATE(2025, 0, 1) = December 1, 2024. Very useful for dynamic date calculations.

questions.q: How do you get the last day of the current month?
questions.explanation: Both EOMONTH(TODAY(), 0) and DATE(YEAR(TODAY()), MONTH(TODAY())+1, 0) work! Day 0 of the next month = last day of current month. EOMONTH is simpler if available.

questions.q: You need to count business days (Mon–Fri) remaining until a deadline in A2. Which formula is correct?
questions.explanation: NETWORKDAYS(start, end) counts working days including both start and end dates. Subtract 1 to exclude today and count only REMAINING days. You can also pass a holidays range as 3rd argument.
MONTH extracts the month number from a date. DATE(2025, 8, 15) is August 15, 2025, so MONTH returns 8.
YEAR extracts the 4-digit year from a date. YEAR(TODAY()) gives the current year number.
EOMONTH with 0 returns the last day of the SAME month as the start date. February 2025 has 28 days (not a leap year), so it returns February 28, 2025.
With return_type=2, WEEKDAY assigns: Monday=1, Tuesday=2, Wednesday=3, Thursday=4, Friday=5, Saturday=6, Sunday=7. So 6 = Saturday.

options: The 5th working day of the month
Error — TODAY() needs a fixed date
questions.explanation: WORKDAY calculates a future date by counting only business days (Mon–Fri), skipping weekends. If today is Monday, WORKDAY(TODAY(), 5) = next Monday (skipping Sat/Sun).
January 2025 has 31 calendar days. NETWORKDAYS counts only Mon–Fri. January 2025 has 23 working days (31 days minus 4 Saturdays and 4 Sundays).
TRIM removes leading spaces, trailing spaces, and collapses internal multiple spaces to single spaces. Result: "Hello World" — one space between words, none at edges.
PROPER capitalizes the first letter of each word and lowercases the rest. Great for fixing ALL-CAPS or all-lowercase name data.
EXACT is case-sensitive. 'A' ≠ 'a', so the strings are not identical. Regular = would return TRUE because it ignores case. Use EXACT when case matters.

questions.q: Data pasted from a website has invisible characters causing VLOOKUP to fail. What's the best fix?
questions.explanation: TRIM handles extra spaces; CLEAN removes non-printable characters (ASCII 0-31). Combining =TRIM(CLEAN(A2)) handles both issues at once — the ultimate data cleaning combo.

questions.q: You want to do a case-sensitive count of exactly 'Apple' (not 'apple' or 'APPLE'). Which works?
questions.explanation: COUNTIF is NOT case-sensitive. SUMPRODUCT with EXACT is the workaround: EXACT returns TRUE/FALSE for each cell, multiplying by 1 converts to 1/0, and SUMPRODUCT sums them.

questions.q: A cell has a space character ' ' in it (not empty). What does ISBLANK() return for it?
questions.explanation: ISBLANK only returns TRUE for completely empty cells. A space is still content, so ISBLANK returns FALSE. This is a common gotcha when checking for 'empty' data.

questions.q: Data from the web has non-breaking spaces (CHAR(160)) that TRIM doesn't remove. How do you fix it?
questions.explanation: TRIM only removes regular spaces (ASCII 32). Non-breaking spaces (CHAR(160)) from HTML must first be replaced with regular spaces using SUBSTITUTE, then TRIM can clean them up.
LEFT extracts from the beginning of text. The first 3 characters of "Singapore" are "Sin".
RIGHT extracts from the end of text. The last 4 characters of "Report.xlsx" are "xlsx".
LEN counts ALL characters including spaces. "Hello World" has 5 + 1 space + 5 = 11 characters.
MID starts at position 4 and takes 4 characters. Position 4 of "ID-2025-001" is '2', so MID returns "2025".
SUBSTITUTE finds "World" in the text and replaces it with "Excel", giving "Hello Excel".
With ignore_empty=TRUE, TEXTJOIN skips empty strings. So it joins just "Alice" and "Bob" with ", " to give "Alice, Bob".
UPPER converts all letters to uppercase. "hello" becomes "HELLO".
VALUE cannot handle leading/trailing spaces — it returns #VALUE! error. The fix is =VALUE(TRIM("  42  ")) which first removes spaces, then converts to number.
TEXT formats a number using a format code. "$#,##0.00" adds a dollar sign, thousands separators (commas), and 2 decimal places. Result: "$1,234,567.80".

options: Counts how many commas are in A2
Returns TRUE if a comma exists
questions.explanation: Classic trick: SUBSTITUTE removes all commas, giving shorter text. Subtracting the shorter length from the original length = the number of commas removed = count of commas.

options: Only the rows where column B says 'Active'
questions.explanation: FILTER returns a new array containing only the rows that match the condition. It 'spills' all matching rows automatically into the cells below and to the right.

questions.q: In FILTER, how do you combine TWO conditions where BOTH must be true (AND logic)?
questions.explanation: In FILTER array logic: multiply (*) = AND (both must be 1/true), add (+) = OR (at least one must be 1/true). So (B:B="East")*(C:C>1000) returns 1 only where both conditions are met.

options: Stacks arrays on top of each other (vertically)
questions.explanation: VSTACK(array1, array2, ...) stacks arrays VERTICALLY — array2 goes below array1. For horizontal stacking, use HSTACK. Perfect for combining monthly data tables.

questions.q: Which formula is available in Google Sheets but NOT in Excel?
questions.explanation: QUERY is a Google Sheets exclusive that lets you run SQL-like queries on your data. FILTER, XLOOKUP, and VSTACK are all available in modern Excel.

questions.q: What is the main benefit of using LET() in a formula?
options: It makes formulas run in the background
It names repeated sub-calculations so they're computed once and reused
It lets you write formulas across multiple cells
It allows formulas to reference other workbooks
questions.explanation: LET(name, value, ..., result) lets you define named variables inside a formula. If a calculation is used 3+ times, LET it! Excel/Sheets calculates it once and reuses the result — cleaner AND faster.

questions.q: =IMPORTRANGE(url, "Sales!A2:D100") — what does this require the first time it's used?
options: The source sheet must be public
You must click 'Allow access' to authorize the connection
Both sheets must be in the same Google account
The source range must have headers
questions.explanation: Google Sheets requires explicit authorization when IMPORTRANGE first connects to another spreadsheet. A prompt appears asking you to 'Allow access' — this is a one-time permission per spreadsheet pair.

questions.q: =FILTER(VSTACK(Jan!A:C, Feb!A:C, Mar!A:C), VSTACK(Jan!B:B, Feb!B:B, Mar!B:B)="Active") — what does this achieve?
options: Combines all three months into one table, then filters to show only 'Active' rows
This formula would cause an error
questions.explanation: VSTACK combines the three monthly ranges into one big table. The second VSTACK creates the matching condition array. FILTER then applies the condition to the combined data — all in one formula.

questions.q: Why is INDIRECT() considered a 'volatile' function?
options: It recalculates every time ANY cell in the workbook changes, even unrelated ones
It only works in volatile (online) spreadsheets
questions.explanation: Volatile functions recalculate on EVERY change in the workbook, not just when their dependencies change. INDIRECT, TODAY, NOW, RAND, and OFFSET are all volatile. Use sparingly in large models as they slow everything down.

options: Ascending (A to Z, small to large)
Descending (Z to A, large to small)
questions.explanation: In SORT, sort_order -1 means descending (largest to smallest, Z to A). sort_order 1 means ascending.

questions.q: =UNIQUE(A2:A100) — what does it return if a value appears 5 times?
options: The value with a count of 5
questions.explanation: UNIQUE returns each distinct value exactly once, regardless of how many times it appears in the original data. 5 duplicates become 1 unique entry.

options: An error — SEQUENCE only takes 2 arguments
questions.explanation: SEQUENCE(rows, columns, start, step): 3 rows, 3 columns, starting at 1, stepping by 1. This creates a 3×3 grid: 1,2,3 / 4,5,6 / 7,8,9.

questions.q: =SORTBY(A2:A10, B2:B10, -1) sorts column A based on:
questions.explanation: SORTBY sorts the first array (A2:A10) using another array as the sort key (B2:B10). The -1 means descending. The key doesn't have to be in the sorted range.

questions.q: What is a LAMBDA function primarily used for?
options: Defining reusable custom functions within formulas
questions.explanation: LAMBDA lets you create custom, reusable functions without VBA or macros. Name it in the Name Manager (Excel) or Named Functions (Sheets) and call it like any built-in formula throughout your workbook.
RANDARRAY(rows, columns, min, max, integer): 5 rows, 1 column, range 1 to 10, integer=TRUE means whole numbers only. Result: 5 random whole numbers between 1 and 10.

questions.q: Which formula would you use to find the total revenue?
questions.explanation: SUM adds all the values in the range — perfect for totaling revenue, expenses, or any numeric column.

questions.q: Your VLOOKUP is returning #N/A for some values. The quickest fix is:
questions.explanation: Wrapping with IFERROR is the fastest fix. =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(...), "") or =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(...), "Not Found") catches the #N/A and shows something friendly instead.

questions.q: You need the age of a person born on the date in A2. Which formula is most accurate?
questions.explanation: DATEDIF with "Y" is the most accurate as it counts complete years including leap year handling. =YEAR(TODAY())-YEAR(A2) gives wrong results before the birthday in the current year.

questions.q: Which combination is the 'ultimate data cleaning' formula?
questions.explanation: =TRIM(CLEAN(A2)) is the gold standard: CLEAN removes non-printable characters (from PDFs/websites), TRIM removes extra spaces. Together they handle 95% of imported data issues.

questions.q: You want to look up a value but the match column is to the RIGHT of the return column. Which formula handles this?
options: =VLOOKUP() with FALSE as the last argument
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP can ONLY look right. For left lookups, use XLOOKUP (most modern) or INDEX+MATCH. XLOOKUP: =XLOOKUP(value, lookup_col, return_col) — no restriction on direction.

options: A = Sales OR B > 1000 OR C = Active
A = Sales AND B > 1000 AND C = Active
Only A = Sales (other conditions are ignored)
At least 2 of the 3 conditions are met
questions.explanation: COUNTIFS requires ALL conditions to be true simultaneously (AND logic). Every pair of range+criteria is checked in the same row. For OR logic, you'd use multiple COUNTIF calls added together.

questions.q: What does =LET(x, A2*B2, y, x*0.9, x+y) calculate?
options: Error — x is used before defined
questions.explanation: LET defines x = A2*B2, then y = x*0.9. Final result = x + y = A2*B2 + A2*B2*0.9 = A2*B2*(1+0.9) = A2*B2*1.9. Note: y references x, which is fine since LET evaluates in order.

questions.q: Which formula combination efficiently filters a merged multi-sheet dataset AND selects specific columns?
questions.explanation: The power pipeline: VSTACK merges sheets → FILTER narrows rows → CHOOSECOLS picks columns. This is a common modern pattern that avoids helper columns entirely.

questions.q: You have a formula that repeats a complex sub-calculation 5 times. Performance is slow. Best fix?
options: Use LET() to name the sub-calculation and reuse it
questions.explanation: LET() calculates each named variable only once, then reuses the cached result. This is far better than repeating the calculation (which runs 5 times) or breaking into helper cells (which clutters the sheet).

questions.q: =SUM(A1:A5) when A1=10, A2=20, A3='hello', A4=30, A5='' (empty) returns:
questions.explanation: SUM automatically ignores text and empty cells. It only adds numbers: 10+20+30 = 60. This is why SUM is safer than manual addition (=A1+A2+... which would error on text).

questions.q: You want to extract the first 3 characters from each cell in column A. Which formula?
options: Both A and B return the same result
questions.explanation: Both LEFT(A2, 3) and MID(A2, 1, 3) extract the first 3 characters. LEFT is simpler and more readable for extracting from the start.

questions.q: A column of numbers is sorted. Which formula finds the top 3 values?
questions.explanation: LARGE(array, {1,2,3}) returns an array of the top 3 values at once. SORT descending + taking first 3 rows also works. Both are valid approaches.

questions.q: You need to join 50 names from A2:A51 with commas. Best formula?
options: All of the above are equivalent
questions.explanation: TEXTJOIN handles entire ranges natively — one formula for any number of cells. CONCATENATE and & would require referencing each cell individually, making them impractical for 50 names.

options: How many cells contain the word 'blank'
How many cells in A2:A100 are empty
The position of the first blank cell
How many non-empty cells there are
questions.explanation: COUNTBLANK counts genuinely empty cells. It's the quickest way to spot missing data in a column — useful for data quality checks.

questions.q: =SORT(UNIQUE(FILTER(A2:A100, B2:B100="Active"))) does what in one formula?
options: Returns a sorted list of unique active categories
Filters and sorts but keeps duplicates
Returns an error — too many nested functions
questions.explanation: The pipeline: FILTER keeps only Active rows → UNIQUE removes duplicates → SORT alphabetises the result. This replaces what used to require multiple helper columns or a pivot table.
